Retro Game Blog 1987 — 2026
The Ultimate Authoritative Guide

Street
Fighter

1987 — 2026 • From Pneumatic Pads to a $10 Billion Empire

The complete history of gaming’s defining fighting franchise: origin, accidental revolution, World Warriors, and SF6.

⚡
Quick Answer

Street Fighter is the foundational fighting game franchise by Capcom, originating as an arcade game on August 30, 1987. Its 1991 sequel, Street Fighter II, revolutionised gaming with a combo system discovered by accident, sold more than 200,000 arcade cabinets and 15 million software units, and generated an estimated $10 billion in revenue. The franchise has sold 58 million units total as of December 2025. The latest entry, Street Fighter 6 (June 2023), scored 92/100 on Metacritic and had sold 6.36 million copies by December 31, 2025.

At-a-Glance Facts  1987–2026

FeatureDetails
First ArcadeAugust 30, 1987 (Japan); December 1987 (North America)
DeveloperCapcom (all main entries); Ocean Software (European home ports, SF1)
CreatorTakashi Nishiyama (director) & Hiroshi Matsumoto — both left for SNK after SF1
SF II Lead TeamYoshiki Okamoto, Akira Nishitani, Akira Yasuda, Noritaka Funamizu (producer)
Hadouken OriginWave Motion Gun from 1970s anime Space Battleship Yamato — Nishiyama’s concept
Combo DiscoveryAccidental glitch found by Funamizu during car-bonus-stage bug check, 1990
SF II RevenueEst. $10 billion+ (all versions, inflation-adjusted); 200,000+ cabinets; 15M+ software units
SF II 1994 Reach25 million players in the United States alone as of 1994
Franchise Total Sales58 million units worldwide as of December 2025 (Capcom Q3 FY2026 report)
Street Fighter 6Released June 2, 2023; 92/100 Metacritic (PS5); 92/100 OpenCritic; 98% critics recommend
SF6 Sales6.36 million units worldwide as of December 31, 2025
Balrog / M. BisonNames rotated internationally to avoid Mike Tyson likeness lawsuit; Tyson said he was “honored” in 2019
Best for NewcomersStreet Fighter 6 (2023) — Modern Controls mode plus World Tour campaign
SF6 DLC HighlightTerry Bogard & Mai Shiranui (Fatal Fury) — created by Nishiyama, SF’s original director

1 The 1987 Origin: One Developer’s Vision, Two Arcade Cabinets

The story of Street Fighter begins not with a committee, but with a single game designer spacing out during a meeting. In 1986, Takashi Nishiyama — who had already invented the beat-’em-up genre with Kung-Fu Master (1984) — was thinking about electro-mechanical arcade machines. In his own words: “Back in the day, there was an electro-mechanical arcade machine where you did karate chops to break bricks. I was wondering if you could make that into a video game. That was the genesis of the idea for Street Fighter.”

He wanted something “much brighter — something you could cathartically release your strength and power on.” Working with planner Hiroshi Matsumoto, Nishiyama brought the concept to Capcom in spring 1986. The game released on August 30, 1987 in Japan.

The Hadouken’s Hidden Origin

The Hadouken — today one of the most recognisable special moves in all of gaming — was Nishiyama’s personal creation. Its concept was directly inspired by the Wave Motion Gun from the 1970s Japanese anime series Space Battleship Yamato. The gesture of gathering and projecting energy outward through the hands became the template for fireball moves across the entire fighting game genre for the next 40 years.

The Two-Cabinet Problem
The Deluxe Cabinet: Pneumatic Pads

The “Deluxe” cabinet featured two large rubber-covered pneumatic piston pads made by SMC Pneumatics of California — one for punches, one for kicks — returning three pressure values (light, medium, heavy) depending on how hard the player struck. The problem: players regularly injured themselves. The internal pipes kept disconnecting. Machines broke down constantly. The cabinet was phased out.

The Regular Cabinet: Six Buttons That Changed Everything

As a direct replacement, Capcom created the “Regular” cabinet with six snap-action microswitch buttons — three punches (LP, MP, HP) and three kicks (LK, MK, HK). This six-button layout — born entirely out of a durability problem — became the universal standard for competitive fighting games for the next four decades. Every major series from Mortal Kombat to Tekken to Guilty Gear was designed around or in response to it.

The name “Ryu” was deliberately chosen by Nishiyama: it is the first kanji character (龍) in his own given name “Takashi.” Shortly after Street Fighter released, both Nishiyama and Matsumoto left Capcom for SNK, where Nishiyama created Fatal Fury: King of Fighters (1991) — which he has described as “ultimately what my Street Fighter II would have been.”

2 SF II (1991): The Accidental Revolution

Released on March 7, 1991, Street Fighter II: The World Warrior did not merely improve on its predecessor — it invented a genre and revitalised the entire arcade industry. Development was led by Yoshiki Okamoto with a team of roughly 35–40 people and a budget of approximately $2,450,000.

Funamizu’s Original Words (EDGE Magazine, 2002)

“While I was making a bug check during the car bonus stage… I noticed something strange, curious. I taped the sequence and we saw that during the punch timing, it was possible to add a second hit and so on. I thought this was something impossible to make useful inside a game… So we decided to leave the feature as a hidden one. The most interesting thing is that this became the base for future titles… In SFII we thought if you got the perfect timing you could place several hits, up to four I think. Then we managed to place eight! A bug? Maybe.”

  • 🏆
    Highest-Grossing Arcade Game 1991 & 1992
    Topped charts for 16 consecutive months on the RePlay arcade chart. Each machine earned $1,300–$1,400 per week at launch in US test markets.
  • 🏠
    200,000+ Arcade Cabinets Sold
    25 million players in the United States alone by 1994. Total revenue exceeds $10 billion inflation-adjusted — one of the top three highest-grossing Japan-made arcade games ever, after Space Invaders and Pac-Man.
  • 🥊
    Birth of a Genre
    Mortal Kombat co-creator Ed Boon directly cited SF II as the inspiration: “To me, the fighting game genre wasn’t even a genre, really.” Every single fighting game released since 1991 either uses combo systems or explicitly defines itself by not using them.

3 The World Warriors: Complete SF II Original Roster

The twelve characters of Street Fighter II were a careful exercise in national diversity and archetype contrast, designed by Akira Yasuda to ensure every player could find a character that suited their personality and playstyle.

CharacterOriginArchetypeSignature MoveSignificance
RyuJapanShoto / BalancedHadouken — ↓↘→ + PProtagonist. Name from first kanji in creator Nishiyama’s given name “Takashi.”
KenUSAShoto / AggressiveShoryuken — →↓↘ + PPlayer 2 exclusive in SF1; one of the first palette-swap characters in gaming history.
Chun-LiChinaRushdown / SpeedLightning Kick — Rapidly tap PFirst playable female character in a competitive one-on-one fighting game.
GuileUSACharge / DefensiveSonic Boom — Charge ←, → + P“Go Home and Be a Family Man” became a meme. Flash Kick is the archetypal anti-air.
BlankaBrazilMix-Up / GrappleElectric Thunder — Hold punch buttonsFirst character with a truly “beast” moveset; design based on Amazon River myths.
DhalsimIndiaZoner / KeepawayYoga Fire — ↓↘→ + PIntroduced the “zoner” archetype used across every fighting game since.
ZangiefUSSRGrappler / PowerSpinning Piledriver — 360° + PFull circular stick motion; still the most demanding input for beginners.
E. HondaJapanCharge / RushdownHundred Hand Slap — Rapidly tap PInspired “mash” techniques; template for high-hit-count attacks in later titles.
Balrog*USACharge / BoxerDash Straight — Charge ←, → + P*Originally “M. Bison” in Japan (Mike Tyson likeness). Names rotated internationally to avoid lawsuit.
VegaSpainRushdown / PokeRolling Crystal Flash — Charge ←, → + POnly character with a weapon by default; wall-jumping introduced verticality to fighting games.
SagatThailandCharge / ZonerTiger Shot — ↓↘→ + PFinal boss of SF1; chest scar from Ryu’s Shoryuken. Iconic rival design.
M. BisonUnknownCharge / RushdownPsycho Crusher — Charge ←, → + P+KKnown as “Vega” in Japan. The series’ defining villain.
The Balrog / Vega / M. Bison Name Rotation — Explained

In the original Japanese version, the boss characters were named: M. Bison (boxer — modelled on Mike Tyson), Balrog (claw-wielding Spaniard), and Vega (the dictator final boss). For international release, Capcom rotated all three names to avoid a Mike Tyson likeness infringement lawsuit. In a 2019 interview, Tyson himself was asked about the design and said he was “honored by the impersonation.” The name swap created four decades of fandom confusion.

4 Special Moves Reference: Every Iconic Input Explained

Command-based special moves — inputs requiring specific joystick movements before pressing a button — were revolutionary in 1987. No prior game had tied powerful attacks to controller sequences. The system encouraged discovery, mastery, and community knowledge-sharing that defines fighting game culture to this day.

MoveJapaneseInputCharacter(s)Strategy
Hadouken波動拳 (Wave-Motion Fist)↓↘→ + PRyu, Ken, AkumaZoning projectile. Light version travels slowly for corner pressure; heavy crosses the screen instantly.
Shoryuken昇龍拳 (Rising Dragon Punch)→↓↘ + PRyu, Ken, AkumaDefinitive anti-air. Invincible on startup; punishable on block. High risk, high reward.
Tatsumaki竜巻旋風脚 (Tornado Kick)↓↙← + KRyu, KenCross-up and approach tool. Light version stays in place; heavy travels forward.
Sonic BoomソニックブームCharge ←, → + PGuileCharge move. Guile’s default stance already charges back. Layer with Flash Kick for zoning.
Flash KickサマーソルトキックCharge ↓, ↑ + KGuileBest anti-air in the game. Invincible from frame 1 on classic version. Forces opponents to respect jump-ins.
Spinning Piledriverスクリューパイルドライバー360° + PZangiefHardest normal input in all of SF. Common shortcut: start at ↓, complete full circle. Massive damage.
Spinning Bird KickスピニングバードキックCharge ↓, ↑ + KChun-LiCharge anti-air; also used mid-combo. Chun-Li’s fastest overhead option in several SF II versions.
Yoga Fireヨガファイア↓↘→ + PDhalsimSlow fireball ideal for long-range control. Dhalsim’s extended limbs and teleport create a full keep-away archetype.
Psycho CrusherサイコクラッシャーCharge ←, → + P+KM. BisonFlies through the screen enveloped in Psycho Power. One of the longest-range special moves in the original roster.
Frame Data: The Hidden Mathematics of Street Fighter

Every attack in Street Fighter runs on 60 frames per second. A “3-frame jab” becomes active in just 0.05 seconds. Understanding frame data means knowing: Startup frames (how quickly the attack can hit), Active frames (how long the hitbox is live), Recovery frames (how long you’re vulnerable after), and On-block advantage (negative values mean you’re punishable). Street Fighter 6 displays frame data natively in its Training Mode — the first time the series has offered this.

5 The Series Timeline: Every Main Entry 1987–2023

  • 🎮
    Street Fighter (1987)
    Only Ryu (P1) and Ken (P2) playable. Three secret special moves undocumented on the cabinet. Pneumatic and six-button cabinet variants. Commercial success in Japan and UK; underwhelming in the US.
  • 🔥
    Street Fighter II: The World Warrior (1991)
    The revolution. Eight playable characters. The accidental combo system. Best-selling game of the arcade’s golden age revival. More than $10 billion in revenue. Birth of the competitive fighting game genre.
  • ⚡
    SF II Revisions (1992–1994): Champion Edition through Super Turbo
    Five major arcade revisions. Super Street Fighter II Turbo (1994) introduced hidden character Akuma and the Super Combo system. Each rebalancing pushed competitive play deeper.
  • 🏭
    Street Fighter Alpha / Zero Series (1995–1998)
    A prequel era covering Ryu and Ken as teenagers. Introduced Alpha Counters, air combos, and V-ism custom combos. More anime-influenced aesthetic.
  • 🆂
    Street Fighter III: 3rd Strike (1999)
    Widely regarded as the pinnacle of 2D fighting game mechanical depth. Introduced the Parry system — pressing forward at the precise moment of impact negates the attack with no chip damage. The mechanic behind the most famous moment in competitive gaming history.
  • 🎨
    Street Fighter IV (2008)
    A 3D-rendered return to the core SF II roster. Brought by Capcom to Dimps — the studio founded by Nishiyama himself, completing a full-circle story. Revived the franchise commercially.
  • 🅰
    Street Fighter V (2016)
    Praised for mechanics but criticised for its bare-bones launch. Eventually recovered through years of DLC support, reaching 7.5 million total sales.
  • ⭐
    Street Fighter 6 (2023)
    92/100 Metacritic, 92/100 OpenCritic, 98% critics recommend. Introduced the Drive System, Modern Controls, World Tour campaign, and Battle Hub. Released to Nintendo Switch 2 in June 2025. 6.36 million units sold as of December 31, 2025.

6 Street Fighter 6 (2023): The Modern Standard

Street Fighter 6 represents the most important entry in the franchise since SF II. It addressed every major criticism of SF V while introducing genuine structural innovations. Its unified Drive Gauge (five segments) powers six distinct Drive actions:

Drive Impact

Armoured attack that absorbs one hit and wall-splats opponents near a corner. Forces opponents to make defensive decisions at all times.

Drive Parry

Hold MP+MK to enter a parry state. Timing a perfect parry restores Drive Gauge — a risk/reward mechanic echoing SF III.

Drive Rush

Cancel from normals or parry to dash forward, enabling new combo routes unavailable in prior SF entries.

Overdrive

EX-equivalent powered-up special moves consuming 2 gauge bars. Higher damage and additional properties per move.

Drive Reversal

Defensive reversal option from a blocking state. Costs 2 Drive bars; pushes opponents back and creates breathing room.

Drive Rush Cancel

Cancel from Drive Rush during normals for extended combos. The deepest execution layer of SF6’s offensive system.

Review Consensus — Street Fighter 6 (June 2023)

OpenCritic: 92/100 — 98% of critics recommend — “Mighty” rating
Metacritic PS5: 92/100 — Universal Acclaim • Metacritic PC: 93/100 — 3rd highest-rated game of 2023
IGN: 9/10 — “The most feature-rich a Street Fighter has ever been at launch.”
Game Informer: 9.5/10 — “One of the most impressive entries the genre has seen in some time.”
EVO 2024: Capcom Cup XI drew 14,000 in-person attendees and 10 million+ online views.

7 The Music: Yoko Shimomura’s Only Game

One of the least-discussed facts about Street Fighter II is that its entire soundtrack was composed by a single person: Yoko Shimomura. The compositions — from Ryu’s sparse stoic theme to Guile’s iconic military march to Chun-Li’s bright, active melody — were delivered under the constraints of Capcom’s CPS-1 hardware.

Street Fighter II was the only game in the series Shimomura worked on. She left Capcom for Square shortly after its release. At Square she went on to compose for Kingdom Hearts (2002), Mario & Luigi: Superstar Saga (2003), and Final Fantasy XV (2016) — and is now recognised as one of the most accomplished video game composers in history. Her legacy began with the World Warriors.

8 The FGC & The Daigo Parry — 2004’s Greatest Moment

*Street Fighter II* created a social phenomenon around arcade cabinets with no precedent. Players put quarters on the lip of the screen to claim “next game.” Crowds gathered. Rivals developed. Store tournament brackets were written on napkins. This was the organic formation of the Fighting Game Community (FGC).

The Daigo Parry — EVO 2004

At EVO 2004, Japan’s Daigo Umehara faced USA’s Justin Wong in a top 8 match. With one pixel of health remaining as Ken, Daigo executed a 15-hit perfect parry of Chun-Li’s full Super Art, then converted into a full combo to win the round. The sequence required 15 consecutive 2-frame perfect parry inputs with zero margin for error — executed live under tournament pressure. The audience’s scream of disbelief became one of the most-shared video clips in FGC history, introducing competitive fighting games to a global mainstream audience.

9 How to Play Street Fighter in 2026

  1. Street Fighter 6 (2023) — PC, PS5, PS4, Xbox Series X|S, Nintendo Switch 2 (from June 2025). The definitive experience. Modern Controls removes input barriers. Start here if you are new to the series.
  2. Street Fighter 30th Anniversary Collection (2018) — Switch, PS4, Xbox One, PC. Includes 12 games from SF1 through SF III: 3rd Strike. Online multiplayer for 5 classic titles. Best legal way to play SF II and 3rd Strike on modern hardware.
  3. Ultra Street Fighter II: The Final Challengers (2017, Nintendo Switch) — Remaster of Super SF II Turbo with HD or pixel-art display options, two new characters (Evil Ryu, Violent Ken), and “Way of the Hado” first-person mode.
  4. Street Fighter III: 3rd Strike Online Edition (2011) — PS3 and Xbox 360 (backwards compatible). Includes GGPO rollback netcode. Still used for online competition by 3S specialists.
  5. Original CPS-1/CPS-2 Arcade Hardware — Required for any official Twin Galaxies world record attempts on the original SF II. Authentic boards available on the collector market.

10 Frequently Asked Questions

What was the first Street Fighter game?
Street Fighter was released on August 30, 1987 (Japan) by Capcom. Directed by Takashi Nishiyama, it featured Ryu (P1) and Ken (P2) against 10 CPU opponents across five countries. It introduced the Hadouken, Shoryuken, and Tatsumaki as undocumented special moves.
Why is Street Fighter II so much more famous than the original?
SF II introduced eight distinct playable characters — each with a unique fighting style — alongside the accidental combo system, superior CPS-1 graphics, and the genre-defining six-button layout. SF1 had only two playable characters and clunkier mechanics. SF II was literally the birth of the competitive fighting game genre as we know it.
How was the combo system invented?
Producer Noritaka Funamizu discovered it accidentally in 1990 while bug-testing the car-smashing bonus stage. He noticed attacks could be linked together too fast for the AI to respond. Believing the timing was too precise for players to use intentionally, he left it in. Players discovered and mastered it within weeks of release. It became the core mechanic of every fighting game that followed.
What does “Hadouken” mean?
The word Hadouken (波動拳) translates as “Wave Motion Fist.” Creator Takashi Nishiyama based the concept on the Wave Motion Gun from the 1970s anime Space Battleship Yamato — imagining the player projecting concentrated energy outward from the hands.
Why was Balrog originally named M. Bison?
In the original Japanese version, the boxing character was named “M. Bison” — a reference to Mike Tyson, whose physical likeness the character was based on. Capcom’s legal team rotated the names of three boss characters for international release to avoid an infringement lawsuit. Tyson said in 2019 he was “honored by the impersonation.”
Who composed the Street Fighter II soundtrack?
The entire original soundtrack was composed by Yoko Shimomura — the only game in the series she worked on. She left Capcom shortly after and went on to compose for Kingdom Hearts, Mario & Luigi, and Final Fantasy XV, becoming one of the most celebrated video game composers in history.
What is the best Street Fighter game to start with in 2026?
Street Fighter 6 is the definitive starting point. Its Modern Controls mode makes special moves accessible with single button presses. The World Tour single-player campaign teaches mechanics through gameplay. The Street Fighter 30th Anniversary Collection is the best route to the classics.
Is Street Fighter 6 on Nintendo Switch 2?
Yes. According to Capcom’s official Q3 FY2026 financial report, Street Fighter 6 was released on Nintendo Switch 2 in June 2025 and contributed to the game passing 6 million lifetime units. The Switch 2 version runs at 60fps — the non-negotiable standard for competitive fighting game play.
What is the total Street Fighter franchise sales figure?
Capcom’s official sales figures list the Street Fighter franchise at 58 million total lifetime units as of December 2025, making it Capcom’s third best-selling series after Resident Evil (183 million) and Monster Hunter (125 million).
